title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Changes in Your Relationship with the Divine
meta_description: Discover why spiritual awakening transforms your connection with the Divine. Honest guidance for navigating faith shifts without fear.
og_title: Why Spiritual Awakening Often Leads to Changes in Your Relationship with the Divine
og_description: Discover why spiritual awakening transforms your connection with the Divine. Honest guidance for navigating faith shifts without fear.
body:
Why Your Relationship with the Divine Is Shifting (And Why That's Okay)

You know that feeling when you're sitting in a place that used to feel like home - maybe a church, a meditation circle, or even just your own prayer corner - and something feels... off? Like the words don't quite land the way they used to? Like the God you were raised with has become a stranger, or worse, like you're the stranger now?

That's not a crisis of faith. That's a spiritual awakening doing its work. And it's one of the most disorienting, lonely, and ultimately freeing experiences you'll ever have.

Here's what nobody tells you: when you wake up spiritually, your relationship with the Divine has to change. It's not a bug - it's a feature. The container you built for your faith when you were younger, more scared, more programmed? That container wasn't built to hold who you're becoming.

Let's talk about what's actually happening, and why it's not something to fix - it's something to surrender to.

The God You Outgrew

Most of us were handed a version of the Divine that fit the people who gave it to us. Maybe it was a God who judged, rewarded, punished, and kept score. Maybe it was a cosmic parent who had a plan for everything, including your suffering. Maybe it was a distant energy that didn't really care about the details of your life.

Whatever shape it took, that version served you at one point. It gave you structure, comfort, or at least a approach to make sense of chaos. But here's the thing about spiritual awakening - it cracks open every approach you've ever built. It doesn't do it to be cruel. It does it because the approach was too small for the truth you're ready to hold.

You start asking questions that used to feel dangerous. Questions like: "What if God isn't a person but a presence?" "What if the Divine doesn't have a plan but an invitation?" "What if I'm not broken and in need of saving, but whole and in need of remembering?"

And those questions? They're not heresy. They're growth. Right?!

The version of the Divine you're outgrowing wasn't wrong - it was just a stage. Like training wheels on a bike. You don't curse the training wheels for limiting you. You thank them for holding you up until you could ride on your own.

The Bright Wound of Disconnection

Here's the part that hurts: when your relationship with the Divine shifts, you often feel profoundly alone. The prayers that used to flow don't come anymore. The rituals that grounded you feel hollow. You might look at people who still believe the old way and feel a mix of envy and distance. You might wonder if you've lost your faith entirely.

You haven't. You're just in the gap between the old and the new. And the gap is a holy place, even when it feels like a wasteland.

I call this "the bright wound" - that raw, exposed space where your old certainties have been stripped away and the new ones haven't formed yet. It's bright because it's full of potential. It's a wound because it hurts like hell to live in.

Your spiritual awakening doesn't take you away from the Divine - it takes you through your old ideas about the Divine, into something so much bigger that your old container simply couldn't hold it.

That's the core insight. You're not losing God. You're losing your idea of God. And ideas are not the same as the real thing. They're maps, not the territory. When the map stops matching the world, you don't burn the world - you get a new map.

Three Ways Your Relationship with the Divine Changes

1. From External to Internal

Early in most people's spiritual lives, the Divine is "out there" - in heaven, in a holy book, in a church, in a guru. You reach toward it. You petition it. You try to earn its favor or avoid its wrath.

But as you wake up, something shifts. You start to sense the Divine within you. Not as a concept, but as a felt presence. You realize that the voice you thought was your own intuition, your own conscience, your own deepest knowing - that voice has been the Divine all along, just wearing a familiar costume.

This is disorienting because it means you can't outsource your connection anymore. You can't just show up on Sunday and get your spiritual fix. You have to learn to listen internally. You have to trust your own direct experience over someone else's authority.

And that's terrifying for people who were taught not to trust themselves. But it's also the most empowering shift you'll ever make. Because once you know the Divine lives in you, no one can take that away. No institution, no dogma, no crisis of faith.

2. From Transactional to Relational

Old model spirituality is often transactional. "If I pray this prayer, I get this result." "If I follow these rules, I earn this reward." "If I suffer well, I get points in the afterlife." It's a spiritual economy based on exchange.

Awakening breaks that economy. You start to realize that the Divine doesn't operate on a points system. You can't bargain your way into grace. You can't perform your way into love. You can't avoid suffering by being good enough.

Instead, you're invited into relationship. A real one. One where you show up as you are - messy, confused, angry, doubtful - and find that you're still held. Still loved. Still welcome.

This is harder than the transactional model, honestly. Because transaction gives you control. Relationship requires vulnerability. You have to let go of the illusion that you can manage the Divine. You have to trust that you're held even when you can't see the hands holding you.

3. From Certainty to Mystery

This might be the hardest shift of all. Most of us were raised to believe that faith means certainty. "I know that I know that I know." But spiritual awakening reveals that certainty is often just a defense against the unknown. It's a way of managing fear by pretending you have all the answers.

Real relationship with the Divine doesn't require certainty. It requires trust. And trust is different. Trust can coexist with not knowing. Trust says, "I don't understand what's happening, but I'm not alone in it."

You start to make peace with questions that don't have tidy answers. Why do children get cancer? Why do prayers go unanswered? Why does suffering fall so unevenly? You stop needing a God who can explain it all and start opening to a presence that can hold it all.

This is where mysticism lives - in the not-knowing. In the dark. In the silence between words. And it's rich. It's deep. It's real in a way that easy answers never were.

Does that land? I hope so, because this is the part most spiritual teachers skip. They want to give you a new package, a new set of beliefs, a new map. But the real work is learning to be okay without a map. Learning to work through by felt sense and inner knowing rather than by a fixed destination.

What to Do When You're in the Gap

So you're in that uncomfortable in-between place. The old relationship with the Divine doesn't work anymore. The new one hasn't fully formed. What do you do?

First, stop trying to force the new one. You can't rush a relationship, and you can't rush spiritual growth. The gap is not a problem to solve - it's a process to surrender to.

Second, let yourself grieve. You're losing something real. Even if the old version of the Divine was too small, even if it caused you pain or limitation, it was still a relationship. Grief is appropriate. Let yourself feel the loss of the God you thought you knew.

Third, stay in practice even when it feels empty. You don't have to pray the way you used to. But keep showing up. Keep sitting in silence. Keep journaling. Keep walking in nature. Keep doing whatever opens a channel, even if the channel feels static right now. The connection is still there - you're just learning to hear it on a different frequency.

Fourth, get support. You don't have to do this alone. Find people who understand that spiritual evolution is real and messy. Find teachers who don't sell certainty but offer companionship in the mystery.

One book that helped me tremendously during this phase was See on Amazon (paid link) - it doesn't try to give you a new God, but instead helps you understand why your old one stopped working and how to open to something more authentic.

Signs Your Relationship with the Divine Is Actually Evolving (Not Dying)

How do you know you're not just losing your faith? How do you know this is growth and not collapse?

Here are some signs:

  • You feel more compassion for people who believe differently than you do, not less
  • Your prayers have become less about asking and more about listening
  • You're less interested in being "right" about God and more interested in being present to God
  • You find the sacred in unexpected places - in nature, in art, in strangers, in your own body
  • You're less afraid of doubt and more curious about it
  • Your relationship with the Divine feels less like duty and more like breathing

If these connect, you're not losing anything. You're shedding a skin. The snake doesn't mourn its old skin - it just leaves it behind and moves forward, more alive than before.

The New Relationship Isn't What You Expected

Here's the thing nobody prepared me for: the new relationship with the Divine is often quieter, less dramatic, and more intimate than the old one. It doesn't come with thunder and lightning. It comes with a deepening sense of presence. A feeling of being held even when nothing is going right. A knowing that you're part of something vast, even when you can't name it.

You might find yourself talking to the Divine differently - more casually, more honestly, more like you're talking to a friend than a king. You might find yourself listening more than speaking. You might find yourself content with silence in a way that used to feel empty.

This is not a lesser relationship. It's a more mature one. It's the difference between a crush and a long-term partnership. The intensity changes, but the depth grows.

Another resource that helped me work through this shift was See on Amazon (paid link). It speaks directly to the experience of feeling like your spiritual foundation is crumbling and offers a grounded, compassionate path through the wreckage into something more real.

What If You Never Go Back to the Old Way?

You won't. That's the honest truth. Once you've tasted direct experience of the Divine - once you've felt it in your own bones, in your own breath, in your own quiet moments - you can't go back to secondhand faith. You can't un-know what you've known.

And that can feel scary. Because it means you're no longer part of the tribe that shared your old beliefs. It means you might feel like a misfit in spaces that used to feel safe. It means you have to build a new container for your spiritual life, one that fits who you are now.

But here's the gift: you get to build it. You get to decide what practices nourish you. What language speaks to your soul. What community holds you without requiring you to shrink. You get to have a relationship with the Divine that is yours - not inherited, not borrowed, not performed for approval.

That's freedom. Real freedom. And it's worth the disorientation.

Practical Ways to Stay Grounded During the Shift

Let me give you some very concrete things to do when you feel untethered:

  • Breathe. Seriously. When you don't know what to pray, just breathe. Let each inhale be an opening. Let each exhale be a release. The breath is the most basic connection to the Divine there is.
  • Walk outside. Nature doesn't argue about theology. It just is. Let the trees teach you about presence. Let the sky remind you of vastness.
  • Write without editing. Journal your confusion, your anger, your longing. Don't try to make it sound spiritual. Let it be raw. The Divine can handle your real feelings.
  • Say "I don't know" out loud. It's a prayer. It's an honest one. It opens a door that certainty keeps closed.
  • Find one thing that still feels true. Maybe it's "love matters." Maybe it's "I am not alone." Maybe it's "there is more than what I can see." Hold that one thing. Let it be your anchor while the rest shifts.

And if you're really struggling, See on Amazon (paid link) offers a gentle but honest approach for rebuilding your spiritual life from the ground up, without pretending you have it all figured out.

You're Not Broken. You're Growing.

Let me say this one more time, because it matters: nothing is wrong with you. Your nervous system is not malfunctioning. Your faith is not failing. You are in the middle of a spiritual metamorphosis, and metamorphosis is messy. It's dark. It's disorienting. But it's also the process by which caterpillars become wings.

The old relationship with the Divine was a cocoon. It protected you when you needed protection. It gave you form when you needed form. But you've outgrown it. And now you're in the chrysalis stage - that in-between place where the old structure is dissolving and the new one hasn't fully formed.

It's uncomfortable. But it's also holy. The chrysalis is not a waiting room - it's a transformation chamber. Something new is being born in you. Something that can hold more mystery, more love, more truth than your old container ever could.

Trust the process. Trust yourself. And trust that the Divine you're meeting in this new way has been with you all along - just waiting for you to be ready for a different kind of relationship.

You're ready. Even if it doesn't feel like it. Even if you're scared. Even if you don't know what to call the presence you're sensing.

That's okay. Call it nothing. Call it everything. Call it the silence between your thoughts. Call it the love that won't let you go.

It doesn't care what you call it. It just wants you to know it's here.

And it always has been.
